item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Literally anything that references alchemists working for the Foundation gets an upvote from me. That being said, I'm intrigued by Seward's obsession with molasses, given that it contributed to his immortality. Is it truly a psychological issue, or, as the alchemist hinted at, are there other, more terrible side-effects we've not been informed of?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
i absolutely love skips with alchemical backgrounds. i am leery about the up vote because i don't really understand the entire "molasses" thing, but i haven't read the other skips.

Still, an immortal is a good skip if done correctly, and this one has peeked my interest.

I love the idea of a clerical error happening after the "transition". and i am curious because i have never heard of this twist before.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Once the tale releases, if it releases, I'll probably reverse my vote. And that balance is difficult and I don't fault you for trying your hardest.

As this is, it's still damn good. I personally don't think it's good enough. Thank you for writing it anyway.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've heard this a few times now.

Long drawn out commentary
We get it magnus, you suck, go away

I understand where you guys are coming from. I could use the excuse that I hadn't really grounded the alchemy department at this point, but the fact is, I felt the article was dragging on, and people have two states when it comes to humanoids:

  1. Holy shit why is this so long, who cares.
  2. There's not enough here, I wish you'd gone further.

There's very rarely a middle ground, and it's come up a lot in other skips. Either it's "too much" or it's "not enough".

That being said, you guys are correct that this didn't go very far.

My issue is, telling things in the SCP format is rough as hell for me, as a writer. That's just a personal weakness of my own writing. Unfortunately, people tend to look down on "Set it up in a skip, expand in a tale" for some reason.

I had originally planned to almost immediately release a tale detailing more, but due to the above, I abandoned the timeframe. I still plan on doing one at some point, but there's a serious wall when it comes to doing multipart stories on the wiki at the moment.

The same deal happened with a couple of my other skips. I'm trying to strike a balance between length, format, and story. As we all know, that's hard.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
These are precisely why I downvoted. The characterization here is fun, but there's no relation to the real Seward, who seemed to have been a very interesting character in his time as a politician. This reads like "First Doctor/grumpy old man + molasses, because funny."

Shame, really, because I love the rest of the Alchemy Division stuff.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It seems it should be the other way around. Since, Molasses would make a good ingredient for Peanut Brittle. However the timing is weird.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Was this at all influenced by the experiments of Sir Quentin Trembly?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: how does Jeffrey fit into all this?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Agreed with the above about:

1. Lack of characterization: Sure, grumpy old Seward is grumpy and adorable, and he's definitely one of the more endearing humanoids I've read about, but I feel like we don't have enough of him.
It would be really great to have more insight into his backstory and how it ties into the fact that he's Seward, because Seward historically has no known connections to the molasses flood. All in all, it kinda just seems like random Seward shoehorned in for the sake of Seward.

Even just small trivial Seward-related details would be great. I vaguely remember reading in this article that he requested for cigars (Seward loved cigars IRL) and was denied, but it turns out I was just hallucinating.

2. Nothing really happens: The narrative doesn't progress very much, it's literally just "Foundation agents find a Seward, the alchemists say Seward is immortal because of molasses." While the idea of an alchemy division is wonderfully absurd, there's not very much done with that idea here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm really going to have to agree with Roget above on this. The article doesn't do much for me, and without the object having much of a character for me to grab onto, it just kinda bores me. I don't see the connection between Seward and molasses and the great 1919 flood of molasses. You could replace it with any politician from that era and it wouldn't have made much a difference.

Overall, I had to downvote this. A shame, really. I feel if you gave Seward more characterization, this could be a much much better article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As many others have stated, this is a very well-written article, but the characterization of Seward could be made stronger perhaps by the use of an interview log. I also dislike the crosslink and the link to Wikipedia, though this could be more of a personal thing.

Novote

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What's here is done well, but I'm currently novoting. I wish this went into more detail about Seward's personality and character, since this is only scratching the surface of a potentially interesting person. I was also expecting more out of the addenda, which ended up feeling underwhelming.

item.title: Re: Tag Changes
item.content:encoded:
I didn't add any of the original tags, for the record.

Agreed with both changes.

item.title: Tag Changes
item.content:encoded: Noting that I have removed the 'resurrection' tag (since it refers to a canon, that I am quite sure this article is not explicitly part of), and added the 'scp' tag that all SCP articles must have.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This may not be enough to swing you guys, but there is going to be a supplement soon, which should fill in some of the gaps.

I didn't want to info-dump here, when there was going to be an additional material to fill in some of the "Wat" bits.

Stay Tuned, folks.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I had some reservations, but I think "William Henry Seward, immortal because molasses", at this level of quality, is worthy of an upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The reference to the molasses robot puzzled me at first. Then I read further.

Oh, right. That would be an uncomfortable colloquy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I am baffled and amused. This is the good shit right here. +1

item.title: Re: Staff Post: Open
item.content:encoded: Check my author post for licensing information. LIbrary of congress, public domain. <3

item.title: Staff Post: Open
item.content:encoded: Images used in posted articles must comply with the site's image policy. A source must be provided on the discussion page and the source must be compatible with the site license. The author will be notified after this posting via Wikidot PM and will have 48 hours to properly source the image before it is edited out of the page. If the images are not wholly your own, please post the image's source as a link (or links) in reply to this post and edit it into your first post in this discussion thread.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Crash course World History started in 2012, two years after this skip was acquired by a guy who's seen crash course…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While what you say is true (and there's certainly nothing special about Seward), I found that the ways the departments and people spoke was the interesting part of the logs. The Alchemy Division's mannerisms were hilarious, and the idle conversation in the recovery log was another refreshing break from "oh shit we gonna die".

So yeah, the logs don't add much to the SPC itself. There probably should be one to illustrate how Seward's mind has failed. But that doesn't mean they're necessarily bad because of that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel similarly. The logs give a thorough look at the anomaly, but not much happens. The formatting is pretty as hell, and I love that there's an alchemy division, but there's no real progression to speak of.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really wanted to like this, the setup had all of the ingredients for something I would love, but the logs felt underwhelming and didn't really build on the premise. You could mix and match this with any American political figure from the 19th century and not have to change the article significantly in any way. -1

item.title: Author's Note
item.content:encoded:
So a couple things:

1) Thank you to everyone who read this and gave me feedback. Lex, you're always my perennial format checker, thank you. Dex, Sirens, All the various Eldriches, the contents of [CHANNEL REDACTED], and everyone in Site19 who looked at this.

If you'd like to copy the formatting, feel free to do so , it's all available in the page source, as the Module Css.

<3! Fun fact: WH Seward died in 1872, hence the number.

Image was obtained from the library of congress, at This reference. According to their license information there is no publication restriction and this is public domain.
